coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of cost-effective Si-CQD for effective oil separation from core rock
coredata.dc:creator: Salimi, Farhad
Jafarbeigi, Ehsan
Karami, Changiz
Khodapanah, Elnaz
coredata.dc:description: Nanomaterials (NMs) based on carbon are suitable candidates for separating oil from core rock. Nowadays, the application of carbon-based NMs to enhanced oil recovery (EOR) is far less. The production of NMs based on carbon (water-wet) is of great importance due to the high recovery they create in reservoirs. The purpose of this study was to change the wettability of oil-wet (OW) carbonate rock (CR) towards water-wet (WW) conditions using cheap synthetic materials. In this regard, the effect of three quantum carbon dots (CQDs) on saltwaters has been used. The CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal procedure and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) was used as the initial source of carbon. In this regard, Si-CQDs indicated great colloidal sustainability in saltwaters solutions. Therefore, to determine the morphological characteristics and structure of the synthesized NMs, Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) were done. The stability of NMs was investigated using the zeta potential (ZP). The new compound showed acceptable results at a very low concentration of 100 ppm. The findings indicated that the nanofluid (100 ppm Si-CQDs + Seawater (SE)) reduced the interfacial tension value from 23.34 mN/m to 7 mN/m. Also, the results showed the solution of SE + 100 ppm Si-CQDs improved the oil recovery by 24 % and 23 %. Generally, this paper opens the door for the development of carbon-based NMs with the ability to absorb sediments in the porous media of the reservoir for the efficient separation of oil from core rock. Generally, our analysis supports the use of Si-CQDs as an efficient and economical additive for further oil recovery in carbonate reservoirs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Oil/water separation
Hydrothermal method
Si-carbon quantum dots
Wettability alteration (WA)
Enhanced oil recovery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Crude oil, CQDs, and saltwater fluids
Characterization of CQDs synthesized by tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS)
Interfacial tension, CA, and core flooding

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to thank the Islamic Azad University Kermanshah Branch, for the award research fellowship.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the production process of Si-CQDs using a hydrothermal approach.
(a) EDX image and (b) EDX spectra for CR samples.
Experimental setup for CA measurments.
Schematic of CA measurement steps.
Diagram of coreflooding apparatus.
Schematic of core flooding set-up.
SEM surface morphology analysis of Si-CQDs.
TEM image of the Si-CQDs.
The EDX mapping results of the synthesized (Si-CQDs).
Elemental analysis of Si-CQDs by EDX spectroscopy.
FT-IR image of the synthesized Si-CQDs.
Measurement of rock-oil CA at SE + 100 ppm Si-CQDs concentration.
Sustainability studies of Si-CQDs.
Impact of various concentrations of the Si-CQDs on the CA of the oil-rock-SE system at 298.15°K.
The CA measurement of of SE-saturated CR samples in the SE.
The CA measurement of oil-saturated CR samples in the SE.
Impact of Si-CQDs on the CA of crude oil with CR (saltwater saturated) sample with 100 ppm Si-CQDs in SE at 298.15 °K.
Effect of Si-CQDs on the CA of crude oil with CR (saltwater saturated) rock in three various salinities (SE, low salinity, and FMW) with 100 ppm Si-CQDs in the saltwater at 298.15 °K.
Cumulative oil production versus injected volume from brine and 100 ppm Si-CQDs + SE flooding.
Mechanism of prepared solution (100 ppm Si-CQDS + SE) for EOR purpose.
Chemical composition of the various saltwaters.
Hydrodynamic diameter and ZP of the different Si-CQDs types from dynamic light scattering in DIW, SE, and 100 ppm Si-CQDs in SE measured at different temperatures with an exposure period of 10 days.
Properties of CRs.
ZP measurements (rock, oil, SE, and Si-CQDs).
IFT of the oil/SE with the addition of Si-CQDs in SE at ambient conditions.
Oil recovery using 100 ppm Si-CQDs + SE flooding at ambient conditions.

row.entry: oil recovery to total oil recovery ratio after

head.ce:title: Synthesis of cost-effective Si-CQD for effective oil separation from core rock

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Petroleum Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanomaterials (NMs) based on carbon are suitable candidates for separating oil from core rock. Nowadays, the application of carbon-based NMs to enhanced oil recovery (EOR) is far less. The production of NMs based on carbon (water-wet) is of great importance due to the high recovery they create in reservoirs. The purpose of this study was to change the wettability of oil-wet (OW) carbonate rock (CR) towards water-wet (WW) conditions using cheap synthetic materials. In this regard, the effect of three quantum carbon dots (CQDs) on saltwaters has been used. The CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal procedure and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) was used as the initial source of carbon. In this regard, Si-CQDs indicated great colloidal sustainability in saltwaters solutions. Therefore, to determine the morphological characteristics and structure of the synthesized NMs, Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), and Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) were done. The stability of NMs was investigated using the zeta potential (ZP). The new compound showed acceptable results at a very low concentration of 100 ppm. The findings indicated that the nanofluid (100 ppm Si-CQDs + Seawater (SE)) reduced the interfacial tension value from 23.34 mN/m to 7 mN/m. Also, the results showed the solution of SE + 100 ppm Si-CQDs improved the oil recovery by 24 % and 23 %. Generally, this paper opens the door for the development of carbon-based NMs with the ability to absorb sediments in the porous media of the reservoir for the efficient separation of oil from core rock. Generally, our analysis supports the use of Si-CQDs as an efficient and economical additive for further oil recovery in carbonate reservoirs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Enhanced oil recovery (EOR) is the best solution to solve the problem of reducing the production and lifetime of a reservoir [1–5]. In recent years, the use of brine flooding to understand and observe the EOR mechanism has always been the focus of researchers [6–8]. Despite the recent rapid development and diversity of new NMs, relatively little recognition of their potential for EOR has been reported. NMs are remarkably efficient in oil reservoirs due to low production costs (very low doses) and low environmental impact [9,10]. Recently, limited studies have been conducted using carbon dots in order to EOR. Research has shown that these materials can improve the efficiency of oil sweep by interfacial tension reduction and wettability alteration(WA) [11–15]. On the other hand, nano-particles form ordered structures in the confinement of the three-phase (oil–water-rock) contact region. This ordering in the wedge-like area causes extra pressure in the film and separates the oil drop from the surface. This pressure is known as structural disjoining pressure [16]. Generally, low salinity water is a novel EOR procedure that can be applied to porous media [17–22]. Low salinity water has gained tremendous attention due to its cost-effectiveness and environment-friendliness [23–32]. Recently, the idea of combining two different techniques is considered to EOR. These combined processes cause a reduction in interfacial tension and a very good WA in the reservoir [33–35].

Radnia et al. (2018) [11] checked the effect of sulfonated graphene on reservoirs. The results showed that sulfonated graphene could change the wettability of rock from OW to WW. Saw and Mandal (2023) [36] experimentally investigated fluid/fluid and rock/liquid interactions. They performed a series of tests including contact angle, compatibility, rheology, ZP, and coreflooding. The liquid/liquid interaction due to the increase in viscoelastic properties and the reduction in interfacial tension caused the optimal salinity of 2dSW to be chosen as the optimal salinity. On the other hand, due to the rock/liquid interaction due to calcite dissolution and ZP increase, 10dSW salinity was chosen as the optimal salinity. Thus, oil recovery based on liquid/liquid interaction indicated the lowest recovery compared to rock/liquid interaction. Hendraningrat et al. (2013) [37,38,39] studied the influence of silica NF on the wettability of rock in various concentrations of silica nano-particles. The results showed that nano-particles alter the reservoir fluid composition and rock-fluid properties to assist in mobilizing trapped oil. The results showed well that the increase of hydrophilic nano-particles increases water-wetness and decreases the CA of the aqueous phase. Giraldo et al. (2013) [40] used nanofluid (alumina) for EOR. The results showed that alumina NF could improve oil recovery. Ehtesabi et al. (2014) [41] used nanofluid (TiO2) for oil recovery from reservoirs. The results showed that oil recovery can be improved from 49 % to 80 % by using nano-particles (TiO2). Al-Anssari et al. (2018) [42] used SiO2 nano-particles and saltwater to change the wettability of rock (calcite). The results showed that very good WA has been achieved from OW to WW.

Dehghani et al. [43] designed a systematic research plan including an electrical deposition technique for asphaltene deposition. They used additives such as SiO2, γ-Al2O3, and TiO2 nano-particles and combined metal oxide nano-particles such as CeNiO3, CeZrO4 and CeCaO3. The results showed that the synthesized mixed metal oxide nano-particles lead to the reduction of asphaltene deposition (by using a very small value of nano-particles). A comparison of the effects of these new proposed additives showed that CeZrO4 nano-particles are more effective in preventing asphaltene deposition.

Ahmadi and Mansouri (2023) [44] synthesized ZnO-activated carbon nano-composite. They performed the interfacial tension and contact angle tests in the pressure and temperature of the reservoir to obtain the optimal concentration of activated carbon/ZnO and compared the results with pure ZnO nano-particles. The results showed that activated carbon/ZnO has better results than ZnO during wettability and interfacial tension tests, especially at the optimum concentration of 90 ppm. Thus, at 90 ppm, the contact angle was changed from 56.27° to 39.14°. The oil recovery factor for base, ZnO, and activated carbon/ZnO was 38.38 %, 51.79 %, and 66.68 %, respectively.

Ziaraty et al. (2023) [45] investigated the effect of synthesized SiO2, Fe3O4, and SiO2/Fe3O4 NMs on WA and asphaltene removal in a water/oil system. The stability of various concentrations of NFs was assessed via sedimentation tests and ZP measurements. The results showed that the maximum WA was obtained at the optimum condition (0.075 wt% of C1000 at 80 °C). Thus, the C1000 nano-composite played a considerable role as an inhibitor compared to SiO2 and Fe3O4 nano-particles.

Although EOR procedures can increase oil recovery between 10 and 20 % compared to recovery through water injection or gas injection, there are cost and environmental impact limitations. Thus, it is necessary to carefully study new materials, such as CQDs, for enhanced oil recovery core scale. Here, the focus is on the properties and fabrication of carbon quantum dots, especially the impact of their application on the reduction of oil–water interfacial tension and WA. In this paper, the impact of interfacial tension tests, contact angle, and flooding on the core scale are performed. In the past, the cost of making nanofluid to change the wettability of rock has always brought a lot of costs, so in this research, by creating a new combination and presenting a new and practical method, the needs related to the economic discussion and creating a condition strong water-wet has been developed for oil-wet CR. Thus, this study explores a more environmentally sustainable method and provides an economical and low-cost nanofluid for EOR applications in oil reservoirs. In general, laboratory techniques have been increasingly used in recent years as a surrogate to obtain details for a range of fluids and chemical compounds. Considering the importance of increasing production from reservoirs, the effect of nanofluid materials in the production process in laboratory dimensions and the use of its results for modeling similar production processes can help improve oil recovery in reservoirs. Overall, this manuscript is a landmark for EOR, presenting the characteristics of NMs and low salinity to solve the problem of oil production from reservoirs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crude oil, CQDs, and saltwater fluids
ce:section.ce:para: Tetraethyl orthosilicate was obtained from Merck. For pH, a phosphate buffer containing phosphoric acid and sodium phosphate was used, and it was adjusted using sodium hydroxide (0.1 M) and hydrochloric acid (0.1 M). On the other hand, the oil sample used was prepared from one of the oil reservoirs in western Iran. Oil samples consisted of aromatics (26 %), saturates (47 %), resins (5 %), and asphaltenes (22 %). In this regard, crude oil has a density (0.831 cP) and viscosity (15.41 g/cm3) at the tested temperature of 298.15 °K. In this study, Si-CQDs are purified and synthesized using the hydrothermal method. Fig. 1 indicates the structure of the synthesized Si-CQDs. Generally, Iran's reservoirs contain high saltwater. In this research, three different salt solutions were prepared. The solution includes seawater (SE), LS water, and high salinity water (FMW). Table 1 depicts the saltwater composition of the 3 solutions. In general, Si-CQDs have high solubility and low interfacial tension. In this paper, hydrodynamic sizes and ZPs of the obtained nano-particles were measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) using Zeta Sizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments Ltd). In this regard, the sustainability of the Si-CQDs solutions is reported and screened in Table 2 . Also, the mean weighted size and ZP was determined.

CR rock samples were used in this research. Generally, 6 rock samples were used. Both oil and brine-saturated cores are prepared and studied for their wettability tendency in the rock-oil-brine system. All CR samples were cleaned using the soxhlet extraction procedure and dried at 100 °C for 24 h [8]. Mineral and elemental analysis CR are summarized in Fig. 2 . Also, the petrophysical properties of CR are demonstrated in Table 3 . Half the CR rock samples were saturated with SE and the other half with crude oil under vacuum and aged in the same fluid at 100 °C at atmospheric pressure for 1,10, and 18 days.

FT-IR spectra are investigated by an FT-IR spectrophotometer (thermo Nicolet AVATAR) model was used. Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra are recorded by Cary 100 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence spectra of the prepared Si-CQDs were obtained by an FL spectrophotometer (USA model LS45) apparatus that was used to excitation wavelengths at 355 nm and 446 nm at room temperature. The morphology of the nano-particles was determined by SEM (TESCAN, MIRA III).

The CQDs were synthesized by hydrothermal procedure [46] and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) was used as the initial source of carbon. Briefly, 50 g of tetraethyl orthosilicate was placed in a teflon-lined steel autoclave for 24 h. This operation was carried out at a temperature of 200 °C. After the required reaction time, the autoclave was cooled down and the upper solution was separated by centrifugation for 10 min, and the Si-CQDs obtained were stored at 4 °C.

ZP analysis is a beneficial indicator of the surface charge of particles and is directly associated with the composition used for preparation. In this study, measurements are done with 0.1 g of milled CR sample dispersed in 10 ml of DIW, SE, low salinity, FMW, Si-CQDs in DIW, Si-CQDs in SE with and without crude oil (5wt/v%). Before taking a measurement, samples were sonicated for about 1 h. Wetting (water-wet) plays a considerable role in oil recovery from reservoirs. Moreover, the importance of understanding the WA of reservoir rock is very important, especially when the rock is in contact with NFs and injected with modified brines.
In the current study, the CA of an oil-saturated CR in 100 ppm Si-CQDs + SE solution showed a change from a strong OW status to a severe WW within 24 h.

The purpose of the coreflood experiment is to demonstrate the application of NFs for EOR in oil reservoirs. The flooding experiments are done at room temperature. The core flood system includes different cylinders to transport the fluid (oil, brine, and nano-fluid (100 ppmSi-CQDs + SE)). In this regard, in Fig. 5 , the process of core flooding is reported. Fig. 6 depicts the components of the core flooding system.
(1)
Cumulative Oil Recover y (\%) =
Produced Oil (mL)
OIP (mL)
x 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs synthesized by tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS)
ce:section.ce:para: The structure of Si-CQDs synthetic material obtained from apple oak extract was determined using SEM, FT-IR, EDX, and mapping. First, the diameter of the Si-CQDs synthesized by tetraethyl orthosilicate was measured. Then, the morphology of the synthesized Si-CQDs nanoparticle surface by SEM analysis is shown in Fig. 7 . A TEM study showed that the particles are 4 to 9 nm in size (Fig. 8 ). Also, the mapping and EDX images of the synthetic material are clearly shown in Fig. 9 . In this figure, the amount of elements is well shown. Fig. 8a shows that there is carbon, oxygen, and Silicon. In Fig. 10 , the EDX analysis of the synthetic Si-CQDs is reported. Fig. 11 indicates the FT-IR analysis of the analyzed nano-particle. In this figure, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups are shown as stretching vibrations of 3300 cm−1(O–H) and 3200 cm−1(COOH) and bending vibrations of (1600 cm−1) O–H and COOH (2700 cm−1).

ZP measurements of the R/O/S system were made with Si-CQDs. ZP measurements help analyze the interactions of the R/O/S/NF systems. Table 4 displays the data for different concentrations and combinations. The clean CR in DIW displayed a very small ZP. By mixing the 100 ppmSi-CQDs in DIW with the CR, the ZP becomes more negative(-9.05 mV). These observations are further strengthened at a concentration of 500 ppmSi-CQD. Also, the ZP is measured at −16.05 mV for the CR in SE. This change in the ZP is due to the interaction of the rock surface with Sodium/Calcium/Magnesium. On the other hand, by mixing the 100 ppmSi-CQDs in SE, the ZP becomes –32.60 mV. The rock-SE-(Si-CQDs) system illustrates negative potential (-42.79 mV). Generally, ions indicate a stronger interaction with the Si-CQDs than the rock surface. By increasing the concentration of Si-CQDs to 500 ppm in SE, the value of ZP reaches −37.38 mV. This change depends on the strong interaction between the rock and Si-CQD with the cations in SE. Fig. 12 depicts the measured the rock-oil-brine CA. In general, the difference between oil-SE and rock-SE decreases with Si-CQDs. This indicates that the strong repulsion between rock-oil-SE and rock-oil-Si-CQDs is developed to improve wettability (from OW to WW).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interfacial tension, CA, and core flooding
ce:section.ce:para: Solution of Si-CQDs were prepared at concentrations of 100 ppm to designate sustainability in both high salinity and purely aqueous systems. The solutions Si-CQDs were stored (25 °C) and monitored by UV spectrophotometry. In this regard, UV analysis for the water and saltwater dilutions of the Si-CQDs NFs indicated excellent colloidal (Fig. 13 ). The Si-CQDs sample displayed excellent colloidal sustainability in saltwater (Fig. 13).

Generally, IFT between water and oil plays a very good role in oil recovery. In this manuscript, the interfacial tension of crude oil/water was measured using a pendant drop procedure. The interfacial tension was studied as a function of Si-CQDs concentration at pressure (14.7 psig) and temperature (298.15 °K) (Table 5 ). Si-CQDs form a layer of nanoparticles at the contact line (oil/rock/SE). In this regard, this thin liquid layer forms a wedge film due to electrostatic and van der Waals forces. This approach separates oil from CR. Generally, aging was done for up to 2 weeks (at atmospheric pressure and 373.15 °K) for oil-saturated samples. Also, CA measurements were made at atmospheric pressure and 298.15°K. In this paper, measurements on only either fully oil saturated or fully SE saturated samples are presented. Aging in SE of SE-saturated samples yielded little to no WA by comparison to aging in oil-saturated samples over the same period of 2 weeks [8].

Fig. 14 indicates the impact of Si-CQDs concentration (0–0.05wt/v%) in SE on the CA of the rock-oil-SE system at 298.15°K and 14.7psig. Both oil-saturated and brine-saturated rock samples were used in these tests (Fig. 14a,b). Samples were exposed to the testing fluid (either SE or Si-CQDs in SE) in the cell for 1 h before releasing the oil droplet onto the rock surface. To stabilize the droplet, CA measurements were made 1 h after the oil droplet touched the rock surface. Generally, SE saturated core samples show slight WW properties with a CA of 78° in pure SE. With enhanced Si-CQDs concentration in SE, the water-wetness increased to reach a value of 31° at a Si-CQDs concentration of only 100 ppm (0.01wt/v%). An analogous trend is observed for the oil-saturated rock samples. The wettability in these samples altered from OW (161°) in pure SE to becoming water-wet (63.4°) in 100 ppm (0.01wt/v%) of Si-CQDs in SE.

Figs. 15 and 16
show the effects of temperature and pressure on the measurement of the CA for SE-saturated and oil-saturated samples. With either an increase in pressure or temperature, these have an effective effect on the WA. Generally, in this article, the effect of temperature is considered more than the effect of pressure. The system (SE-saturated samples) displays a CA (82.5°) under conditions 14.7 psi and 298.15°K (Fig. 17 ). When the temperature of this system increased under constant pressure, CA reached 73.6° (Fig. 15). In this regard, the temperature has increased from 298.15°k to 373.15 °k. On the other hand, as the system pressure increases at 298.15°K, the CA reaches 78.5°. The CA therefore appears to react less sensitively to pressure shifts. Similar results can be seen in Fig. 16. With increasing temperature, the attractive force decreases as the rock surface in the SE becomes more negative. The reduction in the CA with increased temperature from 298.15°K to 373.15°K is higher without Si-CQDs than with Si-CQDs. This behavior is almost reversed for the oil-saturated rock (Fig. 16). The Si-CQDs seem to be more proficient in improving wettability, especially at lower pressures. Fig. 17 depicts the effect of using different Si-CQDs, on the CA of saltwater-saturated samples. 100 ppm Si-CQDs + SE is more efficient than the other solutions, i.e. 300 ppm Si-CQDs and 500ppmSi-CQDs, because it leads to a significant reduction in CA. This can be attributed to the specific ions employed in the solution. Fig. 18 indicates the impact of the saltwater salinity and ionic composition on the wettability SE-saturated-rock with 100 ppm Si-CQDs + SE. Experiments in SE showed higher WW properties than ones that were run in either low salinity or FMW. The reduction of CA in the presence of SE and the synthetic solution of 100 ppm Si-CQDs is very high. This is a strong indication of the strength of 100 ppm Si-CQDs + SE as a wettability modifier.

The mechanism of the created wedge film is that the 100 ppm Si-CQDs + SE solution spreads under the oil droplet, that is, between the corner of the oil droplet and the rock surface, and as a result, the oil droplet is separated from the rock surface. In general, the mechanism of oil separation from the rock surface is due to the interaction between the coordinated groups in the structure of synthetic nanomaterials and the elements in the rock structure. The elements in the stone structure and the compounds in the synthetic nanomaterials were reported using the results of EDX analysis, which indicates the presence of coordinated groups, i.e. silicon (Si), oxygen (O), and hydrogen (H) in both structures. The presence of compounds in the structure of 100 ppm Si-CQDs + SE has caused the CR surface to alter from OW to WW state. The mechanism of the solution (100 ppm Si-CQDs + SE) is shown schematically in Fig. 20 .

In this experimental study, a novel NM was synthesized and subjected to characterization using FTIR, EDX, and SEM techniques to ensure the accuracy of the synthesis process. Generally, our research concentrated on the inexpensive Si-CQDs type and aimed to use low amounts (100, 300, and 500 ppm). Coreflooding, ZP, CA, and interfacial tension tests were conducted to investigate the change in rock wettability.
•
The ZP of 100 ppm Si-CQDs + SE solution was −42.79 mV, indicating stable colloidal suspension.
•
The effective 100 ppm Si-CQDs + SE solution caused significant changes in the interfacial tension of oil and water (from 23.34 mN/m to 7 mN/m).
•
The 100 ppm Si-CQDs + SE solution caused a significant change in contact angle (from 161° to 31°). These results created very strong water-wet conditions for the rock.
•
The results indicated that this new synthetic NM (Si-CQDs) can create a very strong pressure gradient mechanism to separate oil from the rock.
•
Flooding tests on cores 1 and 2 showed that oil recovery through a concentration of 100 ppm Si-CQDs + SE provides significant results. In this regard, oil recovery via flooding on the first core is about 24 %, and oil recovery through flooding on the second core is 23 %.

Farhad Salimi: Formal analysis, Methodology, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Ehsan Jafarbeigi: Data curation, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Changiz Karami: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision. Elnaz Khodapanah: Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Islamic Azad University Kermanshah Branch, for the award research fellowship.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An experimental core flooding investigation of new ZnO-Al2O3 Nanocomposites for enhanced oil recovery in carbonate reservoir
ACS Omega.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Jafarbeigi, Y. Ahmadi, M. Mansouri, S. Ayatollahi, An experimental core flooding investigation of new ZnO-Al2O3 Nanocomposites for enhanced oil recovery in carbonate reservoir, ACS omega. 7(43) (2022) 39107–39121.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Asphaltene destabilization in the presence of an aqueous phase: The effects of salinity, ion type, and contact time
J. Pet. Sci. Eng.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Mokhtari, A. Hosseini, M. Fatemi, S.I. Andersen, S. Ayatollahi, Asphaltene destabilization in the presence of an aqueous phase: The effects of salinity, ion type, and contact time, J. Pet. Sci. Eng. 208 (2022) 109757.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The Simultaneous Effect of Brine Salinity and Dispersed Carbonate Particles on Asphaltene and Emulsion Stability
Energy & Fuels.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Balavi, S. Ayatollahi, H. Mahani, The Simultaneous Effect of Brine Salinity and Dispersed Carbonate Particles on Asphaltene and Emulsion Stability, Energy & Fuels. 37 (8) (2023) 5827-5840.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Introducing a new method of using nanocomposites for preventing asphaltene aggregation during real static and dynamic natural depletion tests
Energy Sources, Part A

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Mansouri, Y. Ahmadi, E. Jafarbeigi. Introducing a new method of using nanocomposites for preventing asphaltene aggregation during real static and dynamic natural depletion tests. Energy Sources, Part A. 44 (3) (2022) 74997513.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of modified graphene oxide (GO) nanofluid on wettability and IFT changes: Experimental study for EOR applications
Pet. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Jafarbeigi, F. Salimi, E. Kamari, M. Mansouri, Effects of modified graphene oxide (GO) nanofluid on wettability and IFT changes: Experimental study for EOR applications, Pet. Sci.. 19(4) (2021) 1779-1792.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Experimental study of the effects of a novel nanoparticle on enhanced oil recovery in carbonate porous media
J. Pet. Sci. Eng.
